original_hypothesis.title: Bribe reward token set can be spammed with many connector tokens causing getReward to exceed gas limits and brick reward claiming
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Bribe.notifyRewardAmount() allows any caller to add a new rewards token if tokenHandler marks it as a connector (or it is already a bribe token). Each new token is pushed into bribeTokens. Later, gaugeManager-driven reward claiming may pass large token arrays (or iterate over bribeTokens off-chain), and each token triggers earned() which itself loops over epochs. A spammer can deposit dust amounts across many connector tokens to inflate bribeTokens length, making reward claiming prohibitively expensive or reverting due to gas limits.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even with trusted sequencer/no mempool MEV, a griefing attacker can act entirely on-chain by paying small amounts to add reward tokens. This threatens availability of bribe claiming and can trap rewards in the bribe contract.
deployment_assumptions: TokenHandler governance whitelists many connector tokens or allows connector expansion over time
GaugeManager/getReward call path uses large tokens arrays (e.g., passes all bribeTokens) rather than a curated subset
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Bribe._isRewardToken accepts tokenHandler.isConnector(_rewardToken); connector set is governance-controlled in TokenHandler.sol but no limits are enforced in Bribe.sol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded growth of reward token list via public function

preconditions: Attacker can source many connector tokens and call notifyRewardAmount with small rewards
TokenHandler marks those tokens as connectors (or tokens are otherwise accepted by _isRewardToken)
Reward claiming path attempts to claim across many tokens

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: unbounded list growth and expensive per-token reward computation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: gas exhaustion for getReward loops in downstream callers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cap on bribeTokens; no minimum reward threshold; no pagination in getReward itself

analysis_questions: How does GaugeManager build the `tokens` array passed to Bribe.getReward() in production?
Is there any on-chain mechanism to prune/blacklist reward tokens per bribe?
What is the worst-case gas for earned() * N tokens with WEEK=1800?
validation_requirements: Bench gas: earned() for varying epoch gaps; multiply by token count
Simulate spam adding 100+ reward tokens and claiming
Confirm whether core distribution relies on claiming 'all tokens' in one transaction

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker repeatedly calls notifyRewardAmount for many connector tokens with minimal amounts, causing bribeTokens to grow. Claiming bribes for a tokenId becomes too expensive or reverts, effectively bricking bribe reward distribution for users.
prerequisites: Connector token set includes many tokens; attacker holds dust amounts

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bribe rewards become unclaimable for affected bribes/epochs, trapping incentives
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All voters for pools with affected bribe contracts
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Limited; would require admin migration to new bribe contract or token pruning feature

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing stress tests for token list growth and claim gas

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor bribeTokens length growth per bribe

validation_result.mechanism: unbounded reward token list growth via dust deposits
validation_result.impact_summary: The bribeTokens array in Bribe.sol can grow without bounds as any caller can add governance-whitelisted connector tokens via notifyRewardAmount with dust amounts. There is no cap, minimum threshold, or pruning mechanism, constituting a code defect with DoS implications for downstream consumers.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis identifies a real code defect in Bribe.sol's notifyRewardAmount function. The function allows any caller to add a new token to the permanent bribeTokens array if it passes _isRewardToken(), which accepts any token that tokenHandler.isConnector() returns true for. Once added, tokens cannot be removed. There is no cap on the bribeTokens array length and no minimum reward threshold.

The direct impact on getReward() is nuanced: getReward() takes an explicit tokens[] parameter from the caller (gaugeManager), so the caller can choose a subset. However:
1. The veNFTAPI contract iterates over all bribeTokens on-chain in _addExternalBribeRewards()
2. Off-chain infrastructure must enumerate all bribeTokens to know which to claim
3. Each earned() call has an unbounded epoch loop (WEEK=1800s means ~336 epochs per 7 days)
4. The combination of many tokens × many epochs per token creates compounding gas costs

The gaugeManager contract (not in scope) determines what tokens[] array is passed to getReward(). If it passes all bribeTokens, claiming would be directly affected. This is config-dependent.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment:
The proof_of_signal is verified. In Bribes.sol, notifyRewardAmount() contains:
- _isRewardToken check that accepts connector tokens
- Unconditional push to bribeTokens when a new token is added
- No cap, no minimum reward amount, no pruning

The earned() function has an unbounded for loop over epochs:
uint256 numEpochs = (BlackTimeLibrary.epochStart(block.timestamp) - _currTs) / WEEK;
for (uint256 i = 0; i < numEpochs; i++) { ... }

With WEEK=1800, this accumulates very quickly.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No cap on bribeTokens length, no minimum reward amount, no ability to remove tokens. Any connector token can be permanently added by any caller with a dust reward amount.
Any governance-whitelisted connector token passes this check, allowing the full set of connector tokens to be added to any bribe contract's bribeTokens array.
With WEEK=1800 seconds, numEpochs grows very quickly (336 per week). Each iteration does multiple storage reads (binary searches). Combined with many bribe tokens, this compounds gas costs.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker identifies all connector tokens via TokenHandler.connectorTokens()
2. For each connector token, attacker calls notifyRewardAmount with dust amount (e.g., 1 wei)
3. _isRewardToken returns true because tokenHandler.isConnector returns true
4. isBribeToken[token] is false, so token is added: isBribeToken=true, bribeTokens.push(token)
6. bribeTokens array grows by 1 per unique connector token
7. Downstream consumers (veNFTAPI._addExternalBribeRewards, any integration iterating bribeTokens) face growing gas costs
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack path is straightforward: for each connector token whitelisted in TokenHandler, the attacker calls Bribe.notifyRewardAmount(connectorToken, 1). The _isRewardToken check passes because tokenHandler.isConnector returns true. Since isBribeToken is initially false for connector tokens, the token is pushed to bribeTokens permanently. There is no cap checking, no minimum amount checking, and no mechanism to remove tokens once added. The veNFTAPI._addExternalBribeRewards function iterates over all bribeTokens calling earned() for each, which itself has an unbounded epoch loop.

deployment_assumptions_checked: TokenHandler governance whitelists many connector tokens or allows connector expansion over time
GaugeManager/getReward call path uses large tokens arrays (e.g., passes all bribeTokens) rather than a curated subset
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that address the number of connector tokens to be whitelisted or how gaugeManager builds the token array for getReward calls. The TokenHandler contract code shows connectors are governance-controlled (Governance modifier), and there is no limit on how many can be whitelisted.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is feasible: attacker needs only dust amounts of each connector token (acquirable via DEX swaps) and gas to call notifyRewardAmount for each. The cost is minimal (dust amounts + gas per call). The bribeTokens array grows permanently. With N connector tokens, each Bribe contract can have N additional tokens added. The direct DoS on getReward depends on how gaugeManager builds the tokens array (not in scope). The DoS on veNFTAPI view functions is certain for large enough bribeTokens arrays, as _addExternalBribeRewards iterates over all of them with earned() calls.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The confirmed code defect is the absence of safeguards on bribeTokens growth. Missing: (1) cap on array length, (2) minimum reward amount, (3) token removal mechanism, (4) pagination in earned(). The impact ranges from degraded API performance (veNFTAPI becomes unusable) to potential DoS on reward claiming if gaugeManager passes all bribeTokens. With WEEK=1800s, the earned() function's epoch loop compounds the issue significantly. Storage bloat from many zero-reward tokens is permanent.
recommended_mitigations: Add a maximum cap on bribeTokens.length (e.g., MAX_REWARD_TOKENS = 50)
Add a minimum reward threshold in notifyRewardAmount to prevent dust deposits
Add an admin function to remove/prune bribeTokens that have zero rewards across all epochs
Add an iteration cap in the earned() function's epoch loop to prevent gas exhaustion
Consider separating connector-based tokens from core bribe tokens with different acceptance criteria
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy TokenHandler with 100+ connector tokens whitelisted. 2. Deploy Bribe contract. 3. Call notifyRewardAmount for each connector token with 1 wei. 4. Verify bribeTokens.length grows to 100+. 5. Create a voter with a deposited balance. 6. Wait several epochs (easy with WEEK=1800s). 7. Measure gas for earned() across all tokens. 8. Attempt to call getReward with all bribeTokens and measure gas consumption. 9. Call veNFTAPI._getRewardsForNft and measure gas.
uncertainties: How gaugeManager builds the tokens[] array for getReward() calls (code not in scope)
Exact number of connector tokens governance plans to whitelist in production
Whether there's an off-chain component that filters bribeTokens before passing to getReward
Block gas limit on the target chain (Avalanche based on deployment scripts)
additional_testing_needed: Gas benchmarking of earned() with varying epoch counts (10, 100, 336) × varying token counts (10, 50, 100)
End-to-end test of bribeTokens spam with 100+ connector tokens and subsequent getReward call
Review gaugeManager source code to determine how it builds token arrays for getReward
Test veNFTAPI.getAllPairRewards gas consumption with inflated bribeTokens arrays
